text:
GYNECOLOGIC ONCOLOGY FOLLOW-UP NOTE
Post-Operative Visit — 4 Weeks Following Primary Debulking Surgery

**CHIEF COMPLAINT**
Ms. Marguerite Okonkwo is a 47-year-old Nigerian-American female with newly diagnosed FIGO Stage IIIC high-grade serous ovarian carcinoma (HGSC), germline BRCA1 pathogenic variant carrier, presenting for her 4-week post-operative follow-up visit after primary debulking surgery (PDS). She is here today for wound check, laboratory review, and finalization of the adjuvant chemotherapy plan.

---

**HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS**
Ms. Okonkwo was diagnosed with bilateral high-grade serous ovarian carcinoma approximately 5 weeks ago after presenting to her gynecologist with a 3-month history of progressive abdominal distension, early satiety, and pelvic pressure. Work-up revealed bilateral complex adnexal masses (right 9.2 cm, left 4.8 cm), massive ascites, peritoneal carcinomatosis with omental caking, and pelvic/para-aortic lymphadenopathy on staging CT. Serum CA-125 at diagnosis was 4,872 U/mL. Ascitic fluid cytology confirmed malignant serous carcinoma cells with immunophenotype consistent with high-grade serous carcinoma (PAX8+, WT1+, p53 aberrant, CK7+). Germline testing via Myriad myRisk returned a pathogenic BRCA1 c.5266dupC (5382insC) variant. Somatic tumor NGS (Foundation One CDx) confirmed BRCA1 somatic LOH, TP53 p.R175H, CCNE1 amplification, and an HRD score of 62 (HRD positive).

She underwent primary debulking surgery approximately 4 weeks ago via midline laparotomy. The procedure included bilateral salpingo-oophorectomy, total abdominal hysterectomy, radical omentectomy, pelvic and para-aortic lymph node dissection, appendectomy, resection of sigmoid mesenteric implants, and stripping of right hemidiaphragm peritoneum. Final resection status was R0 — complete gross resection with no macroscopic residual disease. Final surgical pathology confirmed pT3cN1M0 FIGO Stage IIIC high-grade serous carcinoma, with STIC identified at the fimbriated ends of both fallopian tubes. Four of 18 right pelvic nodes and 2 of 12 para-aortic infrarenal nodes were positive. All resection margins were negative.

The post-operative course was reportedly uncomplicated. She was monitored in the ICU overnight and returned to the floor on POD 1. An ileus resolved by POD 3. She was discharged on POD 6 in stable condition. Since discharge, she reports progressive improvement in her energy level and functional status. She has been ambulating well at home, tolerating a regular diet, and managing her pain with acetaminophen only (no longer requiring opioids). She reports some mild incisional discomfort with movement, which she describes as improving week over week.

She denies any new abdominal pain, fever, chills, nausea, vomiting, diarrhea, or wound drainage. She reports no urinary symptoms. She is experiencing some expected surgical menopause symptoms — intermittent hot flashes and night sweats — which are new since surgery and mildly bothersome. She has not started any hormonal therapy.

She remains emotionally engaged and motivated. She has met with oncology social work and reports the support has been helpful. Her two children (ages 22 and 19) have been informed of the germline BRCA1 finding. Genetic counseling referrals have been placed for both children. She continues to experience understandable anxiety about her diagnosis but feels reassured by the R0 resection outcome and by the multidisciplinary team's guidance.

---

**REVIEW OF SYSTEMS**
- Constitutional: Fatigue, mild-moderate, improving since surgery. No fevers. No rigors. Appetite returning, though still mildly reduced. Weight stable since discharge.
- GI: No nausea, no vomiting. Normal bowel function restored. No abdominal distension. No early satiety at present (resolved post-omentectomy and ascites drainage). No diarrhea or constipation.
- GU: No dysuria. Mild urinary urgency (attributed to post-surgical pelvic changes). No hematuria.
- Musculoskeletal: Incisional discomfort improving. No joint pain.
- Neurologic: No neurologic symptoms at this time.
- Gynecologic: Surgically menopausal. Experiencing hot flashes and mild night sweats. No vaginal bleeding.
- Psychological: Anxiety related to diagnosis, managed with oncology social work support. No formal psychiatric diagnosis at this time.
- All other systems reviewed and negative.

---

**PHYSICAL EXAMINATION**
- General: Well-appearing, well-nourished woman in no acute distress. Alert and oriented x3. Appears comfortable.
- Vital signs: BP 128/78 mmHg (well-controlled on home amlodipine), HR 74 bpm, RR 14, Temp 98.4°F, SpO2 98% on room air, Weight 63.0 kg (stable since discharge), Height 165 cm, BSA 1.74 m2.
- ECOG Performance Status: 1.
- HEENT: Normocephalic, atraumatic. No scleral icterus. Oropharynx clear, no mucositis.
- Neck: No cervical or supraclavicular lymphadenopathy.
- Chest: Clear to auscultation bilaterally. No respiratory distress. No pleural rub.
- Cardiovascular: Regular rate and rhythm. No murmurs. No JVD.
- Abdomen: Midline laparotomy incision well-healed, no erythema, induration, or drainage. Staples removed at prior wound check. No abdominal distension. Abdomen soft, non-tender on palpation. No guarding or rigidity. No palpable masses. No hepatosplenomegaly. No ascites by clinical exam (no fluid wave, no shifting dullness). Bowel sounds present and normal.
- Extremities: No lower extremity edema. No erythema or cord suggestive of DVT. Bilateral lower extremity pulses intact.
- Skin: No rash. No ecchymoses. Port-a-cath site (right anterior chest): clean, no erythema or tenderness, not yet accessed today.
- Neurologic: Cranial nerves II-XII grossly intact. No focal motor or sensory deficits on brief screening examination.
- Lymphatics: No inguinal, axillary, or peripheral lymphadenopathy appreciated.

---

**LABORATORY RESULTS**

CA-125 (obtained today, pre-chemotherapy, post-operative): **843 U/mL**
  — Significant decline from pre-operative value of 4,872 U/mL (approximately 83% decrease), consistent with response to surgical debulking. However, value remains markedly above the upper limit of normal (<35 U/mL), indicating residual microscopic disease burden — entirely expected in the post-debulking setting even with R0 resection and consistent with our understanding of peritoneal micrometastatic disease.

HE4: 138 pmol/L (elevated above normal, consistent with residual disease)

CBC:
- WBC: 7.2 K/uL (normal)
- Hemoglobin: 10.8 g/dL (mild anemia — Grade 1; recovering from intraoperative blood loss requiring 2 units pRBC intraoperatively; iron stores to be assessed if Hgb does not trend upward)
- Hematocrit: 32.4%
- Platelets: 312 K/uL (normal)
- ANC: 4.8 K/uL (normal)

Comprehensive Metabolic Panel:
- Creatinine: 0.80 mg/dL (normal); GFR estimated 68 mL/min by CKD-EPI (adequate renal function for carboplatin dosing)
- Sodium 138, Potassium 4.0, Chloride 103, CO2 25 (normal)
- AST 22, ALT 18, Alkaline phosphatase 74, Total bilirubin 0.6 (all within normal limits)
- Albumin: 3.5 g/dL (mild decrease, expected in post-surgical setting)
- Total protein: 6.8 g/dL

Coagulation (routine post-op check): PT/INR 1.0, aPTT 28 sec (normal)

Thyroid function: TSH 2.1 mIU/L (well-controlled on home levothyroxine)

---

**IMAGING RESULTS**
Post-operative imaging reviewed. Staging CT obtained prior to surgery demonstrated bilateral adnexal masses, omental caking, massive ascites, peritoneal carcinomatosis, and pelvic/para-aortic lymphadenopathy — all addressed surgically. No dedicated post-operative cross-sectional imaging has been obtained at this visit; next restaging CT is planned at interim restaging after cycle 3 of chemotherapy per standard practice. No clinical concern for post-operative complications requiring urgent imaging at this time.

---

**MULTIDISCIPLINARY ASSESSMENT AND PLAN**

**Diagnosis:** High-grade serous ovarian carcinoma, FIGO Stage IIIC (pT3cN1M0), germline BRCA1 pathogenic variant (c.5266dupC), HRD positive, R0 primary debulking surgery completed approximately 4 weeks ago.

**Overall Assessment:** Ms. Okonkwo has recovered exceptionally well from a technically demanding cytoreductive surgical procedure. She achieved R0 resection — the single most important prognostic factor at this stage of disease. Her CA-125, while still elevated at 843 U/mL, has declined dramatically from 4,872 U/mL pre-operatively, and this trajectory is entirely reassuring. She is medically cleared to begin adjuvant chemotherapy.

---

**1. Adjuvant Chemotherapy — Plan Finalized**

Given the pathologic diagnosis of FIGO IIIC HGSC with R0 debulking and confirmed germline BRCA1 pathogenic variant, systemic chemotherapy is strongly indicated. The standard of care for this patient is platinum-based doublet chemotherapy. The case was reviewed at our multidisciplinary tumor board, and the following plan was agreed upon:

- **Regimen:** Carboplatin AUC 6 / Paclitaxel 175 mg/m2 IV every 21 days (q21d)
- **Duration:** 6 cycles
- **BSA:** 1.74 m2; Paclitaxel dose calculated at 304 mg
- **Carboplatin dose:** Calculated using Calvert formula with measured GFR of 68 mL/min; Carboplatin dose approximately 612 mg (AUC 6 x [GFR + 25])
- **Venous access:** Port-a-cath in place (placed prior to today's visit)
- **Standard premedications:** Dexamethasone 20 mg IV, diphenhydramine 50 mg IV, famotidine 20 mg IV (standard hypersensitivity prophylaxis for paclitaxel)
- **Paclitaxel infusion:** Over 3 hours; carboplatin infusion over 30 minutes following paclitaxel
- **Antiemetics:** Ondansetron 8 mg PO/IV + dexamethasone (as above) day of infusion; prochlorperazine prescribed PRN for breakthrough nausea at home; lorazepam 0.5 mg PO PRN for anticipatory nausea
- **Growth factor support (G-CSF):** Not routinely required for carboplatin/paclitaxel at standard doses; will reassess based on CBC nadir data
- **Chemotherapy start date:** 2 weeks from today (approximately 6 weeks post-operatively) — standard recovery window

**Regarding bevacizumab:** This was discussed at tumor board and in detail with the patient today. Bevacizumab (Avastin) in combination with carboplatin/paclitaxel and as maintenance (per GOG-0218 and ICON7 data) is an option for FIGO IIIC disease. However, given Ms. Okonkwo's pre-existing hypertension (currently on amlodipine, well-controlled), the additional hypertensive risk conferred by bevacizumab, along with other potential toxicities (proteinuria, wound healing impairment, thromboembolic risk, rare but serious bowel perforation), the patient was counseled that bevacizumab's incremental benefit in the setting of her BRCA1 mutation and planned PARP inhibitor maintenance appears to be modest. After a thorough discussion of risks and benefits, the patient expressed a clear preference to forgo bevacizumab. This decision is reasonable and consistent with current NCCN guidelines, which permit omission of bevacizumab, particularly when PARP inhibitor maintenance is planned. This choice was respected and documented.

**Patient education:** Detailed chemotherapy counseling sheet provided (in English). Discussed expected side effects including alopecia (near-universal with this regimen), nausea/vomiting (prophylaxis prescribed), fatigue, myelosuppression (neutropenia, thrombocytopenia, anemia), peripheral neuropathy (cumulative with paclitaxel, important to report early), hypersensitivity reactions (more common with carboplatin after multiple exposures — to be monitored), and effects on fertility (not applicable, surgical menopause). The patient verbalized understanding. Chemotherapy education nurse will meet with patient before cycle 1.

---

**2. Post-Chemotherapy Maintenance — Plan Discussed**

Given the germline BRCA1 pathogenic variant with confirmed somatic LOH (biallelic inactivation of BRCA1), HRD positivity (HRD score 62), and R0 resection, Ms. Okonkwo is an ideal candidate for PARP inhibitor maintenance therapy following completion of platinum-based chemotherapy. Based on the PRIMA/ENGOT-OV16 trial results — and more specifically the marked benefit seen in the BRCA-mutated subgroup — the plan is to initiate:

- **Niraparib (Zejula) maintenance** following confirmed complete response to chemotherapy (CA-125 normalization and radiographic complete response)
- Dose will be individualized per FDA label based on body weight and baseline platelet count at the time of initiation
- Given her weight of 63 kg (<77 kg threshold), she will likely be started at the individualized starting dose of 200 mg daily rather than the full 300 mg dose to reduce risk of hematologic toxicity
- Planned duration: 3 years (per PRIMA protocol)

The patient was informed of this plan and expressed enthusiasm about a targeted maintenance approach given her BRCA1 status. She was counseled that the goal of maintenance is to delay or prevent recurrence, not to cure existing disease, and that maintaining surveillance is equally critical.

---

**3. Anemia Management**

Mild anemia (Hgb 10.8) is present and attributable primarily to surgical blood loss and the post-operative period. No intervention is required at this time beyond monitoring. If Hgb does not trend toward recovery as expected with time, iron studies will be obtained. No erythropoietin-stimulating agent planned at this juncture. Will recheck CBC at cycle 1 day 1 of chemotherapy.

---

**4. Surgical Menopause Management**

The patient is now in surgical menopause following bilateral salpingo-oophorectomy. She reports intermittent hot flashes and night sweats, mildly bothersome. Regarding hormone replacement therapy (HRT): Use of systemic estrogen in the context of high-grade serous ovarian carcinoma is generally not contraindicated per most guidelines, as HGSC is not a hormone-dependent tumor in the same way as hormone receptor-positive breast cancer. However, given the ongoing active disease context and the desire to start chemotherapy soon, initiation of HRT will be deferred for the time being and revisited after chemotherapy completion. Non-pharmacologic measures discussed. Low-dose escitalopram or venlafaxine may be considered for vasomotor symptoms if they become more bothersome; this will be reassessed at next visit. Vaginal dryness anticipated; vaginal moisturizers recommended.

---

**5. Germline BRCA1 — Family Management**

Patient's germline BRCA1 c.5266dupC (pathogenic, Class 5) has been confirmed. She has been extensively counseled regarding implications for first-degree relatives (each child has 50% probability of carrying the same variant). Genetic counseling referral placed for both her children (ages 22 and 19). Prophylactic risk-reducing salpingo-oophorectomy (RRSO) for at-risk female family members was discussed as an important option when appropriate (typically deferred until after childbearing, but typically recommended by age 35-40 in BRCA1 carriers). Referral for cascade testing of at-risk first-degree relatives also discussed.

---

**6. Hypertension and Medical Comorbidities**

- Hypertension: BP well-controlled today at 128/78 mmHg on amlodipine. Continue current regimen. Will monitor blood pressure each chemotherapy visit.
- Hypothyroidism: TSH 2.1 mIU/L — well-controlled on levothyroxine. Continue current dose.
- DVT prophylaxis: Enoxaparin was initiated post-operatively. Will discuss transition to pharmacologic prophylaxis approach during outpatient chemotherapy; general oncology recommendations for VTE prophylaxis in high-risk ovarian cancer patients will be reviewed (cancer-associated thrombosis risk high; LMWH or DOAC prophylaxis during treatment per ASCO guidelines may be appropriate — to be discussed at next visit or prior to cycle 1).

---

**7. Psychosocial Support**

Ongoing referral to oncology social work active. Patient reports the support has been helpful in processing the diagnosis and surgical experience. Family has been supportive. She is eager to "get started with treatment" and feels she understands the plan well. Anxiety appropriately contextualized — she is coping well. No referral to psychiatry needed at this time.

---

**FOLLOW-UP PLAN**
- Chemotherapy cycle 1 (carboplatin/paclitaxel): Approximately 2 weeks from today
- CBC to be rechecked at cycle 1 day 1
- CA-125, HE4, CMP at cycle 1 day 1 (baseline pre-chemo)
- Interim restaging CT abdomen/pelvis with contrast after cycle 3
- Routine clinical follow-up visit after cycle 2 to assess tolerability and toxicity
- Genetic counseling appointments confirmed for both children
- Return to clinic PRN for any urgent concerns in the interim

---

Document electronically signed.
Attending Gynecologic Oncologist: [Electronically signed]
Resident/Fellow co-signature: [Electronically signed]
